1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - HR"
     3→part: 16
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "2918-2932"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**HUMAN RESOURCES (HR)**
     9→
    10→**Role of Human Resources in a corporate**  
    11→Human Resources department plays a crucial role in managing people, an organization’s most precious and valuable asset. It oversees recruitment, employee relations, training and compliance. It facilitates attracting, selecting and recruiting talent through job advertisements, screening prospective candidates and conducting interviews.
    12→
    13→HR also develops and implements performance appraisal systems to assess performance of employees mid-year and on year ends. To ensure good employee relations, it addresses workplace issues, conflicts and griviences.
    14→
    15→HR also manages payroll, employee benefits, compensation structures and retirement benefits. It also rolls out the rules and regulations of an organisation. HR ensures that the organisation complies with all the regulatory and legal procedures and abides by labour laws. It handles employee records and creates equal employment opportunities. It forecasts future hiring needs and succession planning in line with vision of the organisation. 
    16→
    17→You have seen, while I discussed on “People” in Part 2 of the book that selecting the best people in an organisation is the toughest job of HR. For example, you need to choose the best employee to win tenders and be number one amongst stiff competitors today and you know how difficult it is to determine the best who would be better than the best of the competitors.
    18→
    19→An organisation has so many departments, namely Strategy, HR, Administration, Marketing, Advertising, Bidding & Costing, Project Management, Site Management, Supply Chain Management, Fleet Management, IT, Technology, Designs & Engineering, Learning & Development, Finance, Accounts, Commercial, Audit, Insurance, Quality, Safety, Ethics and Sustainability. Each department requires people with different sets of soft and hard skills and experience to their credit. HR Head must be aware of these required skills and experience to choose the right people who can deliver the best.
    20→
    21→In many large organisations, if any such organisation does not deliver satisfactory business results in a time bound profitable manner, the HR is taken to task along with the execution team. This is because the top authority believes that had the HR recruited or placed their best people in the right positions, then they could have delivered successfully. Such is the importance of HR. I repeat as I illustrated earlier that human assets are the most important assets in an organisation because unlike other assets, the best talent cannot be easily identified and recruited easily. I would like to reiterate that the student getting 99.5% gets the gold medal. The next student, however good he is, at 99% score he does not get the gold medal, he gets the silver medal. In business, there is no silver medal in a tender where the L1 bidder is likely to be awarded a contract (the gold medal). If you are L2 (silver medal), however good you were at bidding losing to L1 with a whisker only, you are just shunted out. You don’t even get a pie of the contract. You don’t have any sympathy with the employer except for some heart breaking moments with your boss. So you need to be the best producing results. And that’s why HR’s role is very significant. HR needs to recruit the best, not the “good”, so far business procurement is concerned. For some other areas, you may recruit the “good” candidates, not necessarily the best.
    22→

1→---
     2→title: "THE  “KHICHRI APPRAISAL”"
     3→part: 16
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "2933-2977"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE  “KHICHRI APPRAISAL”**
     9→
    10→You must have filled up KRAs (Key Result Areas) at the beginning of a financial year. KRAs are the major goals that you intend to accomplish. Remember, all KRAs need to be SMART and not SMUFE as discussed before.
    11→
    12→If you are a part of appraisal team, you must have heard sometimes “oh, this individual did not do well, however, he has great talent and he needs to be promoted”. There is always confusion in this. Does it mean that if you have talent, then performance is not essential? Does it also mean that if I have performed well, then having potential is not important? HR and some senior leaders are always confused or biased. This is how they should deal with it.
    13→
    14→Firstly, we categorise employees into four categories related to performance and potential;
    15→
    16→a. Performing and high potential employee  
    17→b. Non performing but high potential employee  
    18→c. Performing but low/average potential employee  
    19→d. Non performing and low/average potential employee.
    20→
    21→Let us understand the difference between performance and potential first. Performance is about showing results as per KRA. It is about scoring a goal in a football match. If a top rated striker in a football match plays very well but cannot score a goal, we can conclude that he has not performed though he has good potential. So please don’t get confused between performance and potential. But potential is about the talent, ability, capability to perform. In other words, if you have knowledge, skills and talent, then you have the ability to perform. But that does not mean that you can perform in every situation. Sachin Tendulkar was one of the most talented cricketers of all times, but he could not perform in many matches. 
    22→
    23→Sometimes circumstances are so favourable that an average potential employee can also produce good results. However, low potential employees must always be discouraged. They do not have the potential to perform.  
    24→
    25→Good performance deserves promotion, good increment and bonus, however, potential may deserve only promotion at times. Let’s deal the above four cases one by one.
    26→
    27→a. These are the best employees of your organisation. There is no confusion in this category. They must be rewarded with good increment & bonus and also promotion as per policy of the organization. When someone is promoted, he is expected to perform at that level. Hence the potential must be evaluated properly before a promotion is given. Higher the potential, higher is the probability for him to perform.
    28→
    29→b. We are not always lucky to perform. Sometimes very good students too fail to crack the IIT or IIM. Similarly, sometimes high potential employees known by good performance in the past may not have performed well in the present. However, they are important because they have the talent. So they may be expected to perform well in future even if they have not performed well currently. They too can be promoted based on policy of the organization if the HR feels that one off case of non-performance should not be given high weightage. Such promotions may be given after some years if by and large performance was good in the past years but unfortunately not in the current year. 
    30→
    31→c. We also come across average potential students but have been able to crack a tough exam. Maybe he was lucky to have questions from chapters he studied well. Whatever is the case; an employee may perform well but may lack potential. Lacking potential raises a doubt whether he can deliver every time. Remember, once someone is promoted, it’s difficult to reverse the process. Therefore though these employees are eligible for promotion because of given results, they should be promoted with caution. They may not be lucky to perform well at a higher level if their potential is low.
    32→
    33→d. Non performing low potential employees do not deserve either increment, or bonus or promotion. However, the organisation can develop them to become effective through various kinds of training programs. At least, HR should not be harsh to those low potential employees who worked hard but could not perform. They deserve training. Some organisations may ask them to leave the organization if their performance does not improve over time. But non performing low potential employees who have not worked hard for the organisation simply needs to be told to leave. They have no place in the organisation.
    34→
    35→So, how do we go about it structurally to avoid confusions and not decide increment, bonus and promotions arbitrarily? There are two ways to resolve.
    36→
    37→1. For increment and bonus:  HR may give a 70% and 30% weightages to Performance potential respectively. The percentages can be decided, but performance must be given higher weightage because the ultimate aim of the company is performance.   
    38→2. For promotion: HR may give a 70% weightage and 30% weightages to potential and performance respectively. Remember, in this case, potential does not refer ability to perform in the current level but it refers to the ability at a higher level where he is expected to perform.
    39→
    40→The point I am making is that unfortunately neither the HR Head nor the Leaders make a well structured assessment for increment, bonus and promotions. They mix performance and potential in different ways while assessing different employees and eventually take a wrong decision. This is what a ”khichri” appraisal is about.
    41→
    42→You can set a minimum score to be achieved for an employee to be promoted. Remember that the growth up the corporate ladder is conical. Very little number of employees is promoted at all levels. Hence decision to elevate an employee has to be judicial in a proper structured manner. Also since it is difficult to reverse a promotion case, HR has to be careful not to elevate the undeserving people. Employees scoring below the set score should not be eligible for promotion. They can be considered for good increment and bonus based on their performance. 
    43→
    44→If such a structured process is in place and announced, there can be no confusion and commotion between employees. Trust prevails which is very important in an organization to instill understanding and harmony between employees. Performance appraisals are very sensitive. I have seen employees in the same team subjected to different kind of unjustified appraisals and the team fails due to noncooperation among team members. Hence the importance of a uniform announced structured appraisal process.
    45→
    46→**The salient points are:**
    47→
    48→1. Difficult but possible to achieve KRAs must form the basis of performance  
    49→2. It is important to differentiate between performance and potential  
    50→3. Performance appraisals must be structured giving weightage to both performance and potential  
    51→4. Different weightages of performance and potential may be assigned for increments/bonus and promotions
    52→

1→---
     2→title: "THE 5% A++ MISTHEORY"
     3→part: 16
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "2978-3003"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE 5% A++ MISTHEORY**
     9→
    10→Suppose there are 4 Business Units (BUs) in an organistaion. Say, 2 BU results are poor and 2 BU results are good.
    11→
    12→Also, say, the company decides that in appraisals, they can give A++ grade    
    13→to max 5% employees, A+ to max 15% employees, A to max 30% employees and thereafter the B+, B and C grades, totaling to 100% of employees as per the bell curve. Fine, then what is the problem?
    14→
    15→What happens is that when the methodology is applied to each BU separately, 5% employees of the poorly performing BUs also get A++ and so on. And also ONLY 5% employees of the best performing BU too get A++. Hence a great disparity and commotion builds up across performing BU employees. Why do all the BU employees get equal weightage amidst their vastly different performance?
    16→
    17→However, we must also remember that a BU performing badly does not mean that all employees have performed badly in the BU. Never is this the case. There could be employees with high potential and performance, however, their impact on the total performance of the BU may have been less and hence could not elevate the performance of the BU as such. So, does not such candidates deserve A++ grade?
    18→
    19→The solution is simple. The company can find how many are 5% of the total company employees who can be given A++ grade. Then the company, in its own structured wisdom, for an example, can assign A++ to 2% candidates in one BU, 3% to another, 6% to another and 8% to the best performing BU, keeping the weighted average of A++ employees to 5% across the organization. In this manner, the best performing BU employees can feel that there is a higher weightage given to the better performing BUs. Similar exercise can be done for the other grades across the BUs.
    20→
    21→However, often this is not done. HR must lay down a clear policy to all BUs right at the beginning of the financial year that higher performing BUs will get higher percentage of A++. Similarly will be the exercise for other grades. 
    22→
    23→There is also another problem with the above bell curve approach. We tend to sometimes copy theories without being practical. Bell theory provides guidance to HR but it’s not sacrosanct. If an organization performs very well in a particular year, the A++ percentage across the organization can be decided to be 8% and not restricted to 5%. Similarly if the organization performs poorly in a particular year, the A++ percentage across the organization can be lowered to 3% and not 5%. Similar exercise can be conducted for all other grades. However, in both the cases, the essence of the bell curve may not be compromised.
    24→
    25→Therefore two things emerge. One is that the percentages of grades may be tweaked to different values depending on the performance of the organization. And also these percentages can be tweaked across different BUs keeping the average decided percentage intact across the organization.
    26→
    27→**The salient points are:**
    28→
    29→1. You may follow the principles of bell curve, but the percenatges can be tweaked year on year based on the overall performance of the organization.  
    30→2. The percentages of A++ and other grades may be different across BUs based on the individual performances of the BU, however the weighted percentage of the A++ and other grades across the organization may be as decided.   
    31→3. To give proper justice to performing BUs, the percentages of A++, A+ and A grade employees must be higher than the other BUs.  
    32→4. Though lower percentages of A++, A+ and A grades may be given to low performing BUs, the performing and high potential candidates in these low performing BUs can still be rightly rewarded.
    33→

1→---
     2→title: "SECRET COOKED UP HALF YEARLY APPRAISALS"
     3→part: 16
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3004-3027"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : SECRET COOKED UP HALF YEARLY APPRAISALS**
     9→
    10→Half a year is a long time in corporates. These first 6 months of the financial year build the foundation of performance for the year. That is why half yearly performance appraisals are very important to understand where the organization stands after 50% of the time has passed by.  Yet many companies do not resort to this exercise even if they are integrated in the HR manuals.
    11→
    12→There are some lucky employees who somehow meet their KRAs during the last two months of the year. Should they be rewarded? Some say yes, because they have fulfilled the KRAs. Some say No because they feel the KRAs must have been so easy that they could have met them within 2 months and had there been efforts of the employee round the year he could have achieved much more.
    13→
    14→Consistency is of utmost importance. Consistency provides confidence to an organization on the likely performance in future. You cannot rely on candidates who perform poorly in first 10 months and then meet the KRAs in the next 2 months thereby demanding favourable appraisals. Also, you may not be lucky in this manner every year. The boss needs to introspect how he could Okay his KRAs which the employee was able to meet in just 2 months. In SMART KRAs, the A stands for “Achievable” which indirectly means difficult but possible. KRAs being met in 2 months’ time raise serious questions on being SMART\!
    15→
    16→In order to provide unbiased judgments to all employees, management must resort to half yearly appraisals and may have say, 40% weightage assigned to it, the balance 60% to the final appraisal at the end of the year with KRAs for the first half year being lesser than the latter half. Like in my 4 year course of Civil Engineering, we had 15%, 15%, 35% and 35% weightage in the first, second, third and fourth years respectively and the final marks after completion of the course was based on these weightages. This method ensures consistent performance.
    17→
    18→Why KRAs in the first half may be kept lesser may have some background. Say in the bidding arena, most customers float tenders during the second half of the year. Therefore order book possible in the first half will be definitely less than the second half. Therefore the KRA related to order book in the first half may be kept lesser. It all depends on the work arena and the business environment. You may keep the weightage to 50% for both the first and the second half year if the organization feels that the KRAs for both the half years have to be kept the same.
    19→
    20→Whatever is the case, the subject matter of this chapter is about half yearly appraisals which are often not done. Then during the annual appraisals, the half yearly appraisals of the employees are put in place with cooked up data. Why cooked up? Because the employees already knows what was his performance at the end of first 6 months\! Accordingly he manipulates the KRA for the first 6 months so that the KRAs tallies with the known results and thereby scores well on performance. The balance KRA not yet met at the end of 6 months, but which was declared at the beginning of the year, is mentioned as the KRA for the next 6 months. 
    21→
    22→So, basically the half yearly appraisals are cooked up and manipulated. This does not demonstrate the desired performance of the organization at the end of 6 months as well as cannot determine the desired performance of the employees during the first 6 months. Plotting half yearly appraisals sincerely for all employees demonstrates the “consistency of performance”. Organization does not want desired results all of a sudden in the last two months of a year. Organisations desire consistent performance. Consistent performance is crucial to plan your outlay and put in place the right manpower, infrastructure and finances.
    23→
    24→For example, if you get Rs 4000 Crores of order in the month of March out of your annual target of Rs 4500, then you need to arrange capital, finances, manpower, equipment all of a sudden during the same time which may be very difficult for the organization. Therefore, half yearly KRAs and half yearly performance are extremely important for an organization to plan its resources in a structured manner. A desired result is essential to achieve, but in a systematic manner spread over the year, not all of a sudden.
    25→
    26→**The salient points are:**
    27→
    28→1. Half yearly appraisals must be done immediately at the end of 6 months  
    29→2. Half yearly and annual KRAs must be spelt out for each employee at the beginning of a year  
    30→3. Performance must be based on achieving KRAs in both the halves of the year based on the pre-spelt out weightages assigned for the halves at the beginning of a year. The weightages may be different for different employees based on the dynamics of business environment of the operations of his department
    31→

1→---
     2→title: "MIS-IDENTIFYING FUTURE LEADERS"
     3→part: 16
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3028-3076"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : MIS-IDENTIFYING FUTURE LEADERS**
     9→
    10→Performance, Potential, Strategy and Vision have different meanings.
    11→
    12→Performance is about meeting SMART KRAs within a given time frame. Potential is the capability and talent to perform. Strategy is the intelligent positioning of your company to perform and out beat competitors. Vision is the capability to see what is coming forth for the company just like a palmist’s prediction to an individual.
    13→
    14→They are all important but you need different traits for different position. For Leadership and Future Leaders, Strategy & Vision forms the foundation of the company’s backbone and is of utmost importance for the likely performance of the company in future. Even the best performers in the world cannot achieve results on a weak strategised and visionised platform. Just like the best driver in the world cannot drive efficiently in a defective car. 
    15→
    16→HR and Heads often have wrong ideas. To identify Future Leaders, first and foremost they take notice of the good performers. This is partially a wrong approach. HR must take a different view to determine candidates who have strong acumen on Strategy and Vision.
    17→
    18→There are many examples of this. With due regards to all cricketers, you have seen that some best performing cricketers failed to captain the Indian cricket team. This is because a cricketer may be very good at his playing, but he may not have the overall strategy and vision for the team to win. On the other side, a captain should have the strategy and vision for the team to win, but he may not necessarily be the best player in the team. A poor captain but with good individual performance may ruin the prospects of a game. Same is the case in other sports, politics and organisations. However, I don’t mean that a captain may not have any past performance at all. He should have a decent past performance so that he can appreciate that the strategy and vision formed are not theoretical but are driven to produce results. 
    19→
    20→Suitable tests and interviews need to be conducted for prospective performers to determine their ability to strategise and visualise. High weightage must be given to Strategy and Vision and lesser weightage to Performance and potential. But often it is the other way round.
    21→
    22→I remember, in an organization, there were some good Project Managers (in charge of one or two projects), performing very well year after year and eventually became Project Directors (in charge of all projects under execution). These Project Directors were further elevated to Business Unit Heads. As a BU Head, you need to strategise your business to compete and acquire orders, position your company to sustain its future, present your company through marketing and business development activities for future business. Now, how to strategise to acquire more orders, how to market and position your company for the future, etc. calls for your ability in the fields of Strategy and Vision. The Project Directors, who had expertise to steer projects to completion were not capable to steer the company for the future. Hence they failed in their duties.
    23→
    24→I would like to emphasise that different positions in an organization requires different skills. HR is aware of this but often falters while taking decisions to select their future leaders. The future of an organization will be in deep trouble if their top leaders have a lack of Strategic mind and Vision.
    25→
    26→**The salient points are:**
    27→
    28→1. Strategy and Vision are essential attributes of a leader rather than performance and potential.  
    29→2. Strategy and Vision enables your organisation to position itself amidst stiff competition  
    30→3. Interviews to select future leaders must revolve around Strategy and Vision. Many good potential performers may not have the strategic mind and vision to become future leaders. In fact people with such leadership skills are not common.
    31→
    32→    
    33→
    34→**“TWO” MANY CONSULTANTS SPOIL THE BROTH**
    35→
    36→People say if too many different drivers drive your car on different days; your car is likely to get damaged. I do not know if this is true or not. May be it’s for the following reasons. All drivers have different levels of skills. Some may push the brakes too hard, some may push the clutches too hard, some do not try to change the gear even when the car slows down, etc.  An ideal driver would be the one who would demonstrate equally good skills in using all parts of the car for a smooth drive. Such a driver can be kept permanently for your car to ensure proper usage of the car.
    37→
    38→Similarly, “Two” different consultants in the same arena may be considered as “too” many. I have seen one organisation hiring two consultants from two reputed Engineering institutions for consultancy in Structural Designs. They used to come on different days and guide the design engineers of the organization in their projects. They had different approaches to tackle structural problems, as is prevalent amongst structural engineers. For example, you can provide a 250mm x 400mm concrete beam with required reinforcement but alternatively you can also resort to a 250mm x 500mm concrete beam with lesser reinforcement. The choice is there with the structural engineer.
    39→
    40→In the case of a Structural consultant, one consultant may guide a structure design in some method and another consultant may guide a similar structure in some other method. Debates arise amongst employees as to which approach is good or better. Sometimes, there is no consensus between the consultants and both think their method is the best. Just think if you are treated differently by two doctors for the same ailment, both doctors claiming their treatment to be right and best, what will be your mental condition?
    41→
    42→This is not the question of who is right or who is wrong. If two or too many consultants are taken to handle similar projects, then the philosophy of execution must be the same in order to provide consistency in handling projects by different managers. Top Management must ensure such uniformity in approach by all the hired consultants. While the approach to project execution may be different for different terrains, the principles of design or execution should be consistent. In this way, the dilemma between employees will be removed.
    43→
    44→It so happened that once two projects were running very late. Both the projects pertained to the same customer. One consultant involved in one project resorted to crashing which brought the time of completion significantly down but with a high cost. The other consultant involved with the other project was not in favour of crashing due to its high cost. He thought that the cost escalation for taking longer time will still be much lesser than the crashing cost assuming that the customer will not invoke the Liquidated Damages clause since the customer too could not provide the Right-of-way to the organization at the correct time.
    45→
    46→The above led to terrible debate and confusion amongst employees. With similar projects for the same customer and with similar ongoing delays, the approaches were different.     48→There was also another problem with the consultants. These consultants were retired professionals from customer organisations. During their time, they did not think on behalf of the contractors whether their instructions were practical or not or what would be the cost impact on the contractors to resort to methods suggested by the customer. Now they are sitting in the opposite chairs. On behalf of the contractor, these consultants were responsible for speedy project execution keeping cost in mind. This was not the case when they were sitting on the chairs of the customer.     50→**The salient points are:**

     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE TOP HEAVY BURDEN**
     9→
    10→Refer to the Chapter where Employee overheads have been discussed. You have seen that Employee Overheads \= Total CTC/Total Turnover. This could range from 1% to as high as 7% depending on the type of industry.
    11→
    12→There may be many grades or positions in an organisation for officers, like Asst. Manager, Manager, Deputy Manager, Senior Manager, General Manager, Asst. Vice President, Vice President, Executive Vice President, President, and so on and so forth. All these positions can be clubbed into three levels : 1\. Junior Management; 2\. Middle Management; 3 Top Management. Other than these management levels, there would be supporting staff in the organisations such as the receptionist, office help, photocopy  personnel, drivers, sweepers, pantry boys and girls, etc. All the permanent officers and permanent staff will contribute to the total employee cost to the company, that is, the total CTC
    13→
    14→Now the question is what should be the CTC of the Top management with respect to the total CTC of all employees? Normally the Top management will comprise of very few employees but with very high salaries compared to salaries down below. Maybe this ratio should not exceed 10% but of course it will again depend from industry to industry. You can benchmark yours when you find out that of your competitors and thereafter fix your level. If yours exceed the benchmark of your competitors, then the organisations will be termed as “Top Heavy”.
    15→
    16→A “Top Heavy” CTC is a burden to the company. It not only increases the cost to the company but also sometimes create confusions due to different contradictory directions given down below by different leaders. This was also narrated in the chapter of “The another CXO syndrome” in Part 1 of the book. The synergy amongst “too many” Top Management people tends to get lost with each one of them trying to prove their knowledge, authority and advice as the best in the lot. Just like “Too many cooks spoil the broth”
    17→
    18→**The salient points are:**
    19→
    20→1. Keep a Top light organisation. Fix the Top level overhead cost ratio   
    21→2. Recruit the essential but minimum number of top leaders  
    22→3. Promotion must be few as employees rise higher up the corporate ladder  
    23→4. Compare your top level overhead ratio with your competitors

     7→
     8→**CONTRACTS AND LAW**
     9→
    10→**Role of Contracts and Law in a corporate**  
    11→Contracts Management or Contract Administration is the process of creating, executing and overseeing contracts. Its basic objectives are to:
    12→
    13→1) ensure compliance in line with contract documents  
    14→2) managing risks  
    15→3) deal with customers, suppliers, partners and form agreements with them  
    16→4) file claims
    17→
    18→The entire process of contracts management involves identifying the terms and conditions of the contract, negotiating them with the stakeholder, finalizing agreements and signing them, overseeing compliance during contract execution and close contracts successfully. Contracts managements aids risk mitigation and claims management.
    19→
    20→Contracts Management is an important aspect of Project Execution. Just like a cricket game or any game, indoor or outdoor, is governed by a set of rules, similarly a contract for Project execution is governed by a set of terms called the contractual terms and conditions. For example, Payment terms, in other words, how and when a contractor shall be paid by the client, is an important contractual term in project execution. Like these, there are hundreds of terms and conditions which a Project Manager needs to take note of while executing a Project. All these contractual terms and conditions are compiled in several volumes which is called the “contract”. Upon award, this contract signed by both the client and the contractor is handed over to the contractor by the client. The Contracts manager has to ensure that the project execution abides all clauses stipulated in the contract.
    21→
    22→You shall also have several contacts with your suppliers, sub-contractors, agencies, Joint Venture partners, etc. They may have back to back terms with your organization or as best negotiated.
    23→
    24→Law Management deals with legal affairs within organization and other stake holders. Its basic objectives are to:
    25→
    26→1) ensuring compliance with the laws of the country  
    27→2) identifying and managing legal risks (legal risk mitigation)  
    28→3) form legal strategies  
    29→4) protect organization from legal litigations and reputation  
    30→5) dispute resolution through negotiation and mediation to minimize disruption in business
    31→
    32→In an often litigious business environment that we are in today, effective law management aids corporate governance, reduces costs for legal disputes for the organization and maintains good reputation in the marketplace. 
    33→
    34→Apart from adhering to the contractual terms and conditions, you need to follow the laws of the country. Any unlawful activity performed due to ignorance of law may lead to legal penalties to be borne by the organization. For example, you cannot employ child labour in your projects which is illegal as per the laws existing in India. You need to adhere to different domains of laws of the country like the criminal law, civil law, constitutional law, family law, corporate law, administrative law, tax laws, etc. 
    35→
    36→To ensure the above compliances requirements, a company usually recruits a Contracts Manager to head the Contracts department and a Law Manager to head the Law department. They are very important departments in an organization because any non-compliance may lead to hefty contractual and legal penalties.
    37→

     2→title: "ILLOGICAL CONTRACTUAL CLAIMS"
     3→part: 18
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3252-3266"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : ILLOGICAL CONTRACTUAL CLAIMS**
     9→
    10→During a project execution, Contracts Department may some opportunities to raise claims to a client. However, it must do so judicially by evaluating the financial impact of each claim and raising those which have significant amount of money involved. If a contractor files claims every now and then even for minute issues to the client at every opportunity it gets, then the client too can file claims against the contractor at every opportunity it gets. In such cases the project will be marred with claims and counter claims. Hence claims must be logical, reasonable, significant amount involved and acceptable to the other party. If claims are not acceptable by any other party, then the parties go into dispute resolution methods, which are also stipulated in the contract documents. 
    11→
    12→The Contracts Manager should be capable to convince the client logically on how the organization is significantly hit for a default of the client so that the client has reasons to appreciate and accept the claims. Similarly in all good earnest, a contractor too must accept claims from the client if it finds that the organization has defaulted in a particular case as mentioned by the client. There should not be a war of claims and counter claims between the contractor and the client at every given opportunity which may have insignificant financial impact on the project and time of completion. In other words, the contractor and the client should be in harmony in the interest of project execution and ignore minor disputes and disagreements that may crop of from time to time and solve such indifferences between them in a cordial manner. After all, the goal of both the contractor and the client is the same, ie. To complete the project on time with good quality and maintaining safety.
    13→
    14→**The salient points are:**
    15→
    16→1. All terms and conditions of a project execution are stipulated in several volumes called the contract documents.  
    17→2. Contract documents also stipulate the terms and conditions that a client has to follow.  
    18→3. If certain terms and conditions is not followed, you can file your claims to the client and vice-versa.  
    19→4. Claims should be logical, reasonable and have significant impact financially or in the timelines of the project.  
    20→5. Insignificant claims raised now and then will disturb the harmony and goodwill between the client and the contractor
    21→

     2→title: "NOT BACK TO BACK TERMS"
     3→part: 18
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3282-3299"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NOT BACK TO BACK TERMS**
     9→
    10→Back to back terms mean the same terms that client has imposed on the contractor is imposed to suppliers and subcontractors by the contractor.
    11→
    12→This leads to lot of disagreements. For example, suppose the payment terms of supplies of a client are 10% advance, 60% on supplies, 20% on supplies after the supply material is erected/constructed (ie. Erection linked supply payment) and last 10% retention on completion of project. Now a supplier may argue that since he is not responsible for construction, he will not agree for the erection linked supply payment but demands 80% progressive payment. In this case, the supplier is paid 80% progressive payment but the contractor receives the 20% erection linked supply payment from the client only after the erection is complete, in other words, the contractor is out of pocket and requires finance to pay the supplier first. This is not a back to back term.
    13→
    14→Finance costs due to non-back to back terms have to be loaded in the bid cost. Similarly there may be other contractual clauses of a contract between the client and the customer that a supplier may not agree to.
    15→
    16→Sometimes the CPO/CFO or even the MD insists the Bidding Manager to ensure back to back terms with the suppliers and subcontractors but often this is not possible. The CPO/CFO or the MD is reluctant to discuss with the suppliers because their failures will be evident when the suppliers will not agree to them for the back to back terms. 
    17→
    18→Top position leaders should be practical as to what is achievable and what is not. Forcing juniors to achieve something which they know that they themselves cannot is not appreciated. Leaders must demonstrate juniors on what they can do and not force others on what they cannot do.   
    19→
    20→**The salient points are:**
    21→
    22→1. A Bidding Manager should negotiate with their suppliers and subcontractors to achieve back to back terms as far as possible.  
    23→2. Cost for Non-back to back terms must be loaded in the bid.  
    24→3. Top leaders must appreciate the best efforts made by Bidding team to achieve back to back terms with their suppliers and subcontractors
    25→

     8→**FINANCE & ACCOUNTS**
     9→
    10→**Role of Finance and Accounts in a corporate**  
    11→Finance and Accounts (F\&A) has many functions within an organization, and there are many job titles to reflect the varied job responsibilities. Controllers are in charge of financial reporting and the oversight of the accounting activities necessary to develop those reports. Controllers are concerned with payroll functions, accounts receivable, and accounts payable including taxes, inventory control, banking activities, cash flows, loans and investments, audits and any number of short-term asset and liability tracking and monitoring activities. They aid internal and external auditors and are responsible for monitoring and implementing the day-to-day financial operations of the firm.
    12→
    13→In most organizations, the **treasurer** might assume many of the duties of the controller. However, the treasurer is also responsible for monitoring cash flow at a firm and frequently is the contact person for bankers, underwriters, and other outside sources of financing. A treasurer may be responsible for structuring loan and debt obligations and determining when and from whom to borrow funds. Treasurers are also responsible for investing excess funds. Where a controller may face inward toward the organization, the treasurer often faces outward as a representative to the public.
    14→
    15→The **chief financial officer**, or **CFO**, is in a “big picture” position. The CFO sets policy for working capital management, determines optimal capital structure for the firm, and makes the final decision in matters of Financial planning, forecasting and capital budgeting. 
    16→
    17→Monitoring all parameters of F\&A for each project, each BU, each SBU, each customer, each vendor and the Head Office adds complexity to all these processes but provides a financial view of all business units separately.
    18→
    19→Every organisation works for revenues from within which it earns its profits.  The vision of an organisation may be different from one another but the final financial goals are the same, ie. Generate Revenues and earn profits. The F\&A may not be directly responsible for the revenues and profits of the company but their monitoring, controlling and strategising all financial activities  indirectly supports the financial goals of an organization. Therefore the role of F\&A cannot be over emphasized.
    20→

     8→**CHAPTER : CROSS ALLOTTMENT OF SBU FUNDS**
     9→
    10→Each SBU in an organisation is engaged with a particular kind of business. There may be many SBUs in an organisation. Sometimes it so happens that a particular SBU is doing well financially whereas another SBU is not performing well, rather incurring losses or earling revenues late due to client payment terms. In such a case, the CFO then tries to transfer funds from the SBUs earning well and receiving payments from clients on time to the SBUs incurring losses. By such actions, the Director ruins the business of the SBUs who were performing well. I will explain how.
    11→
    12→Assume C1 is the direct cost of materials and services in a project and C2 is the indirect cost, ie. the project management costs including site management costs and overhead costs of the company; then the total cost of the project, say C \= C1 \+ C2. Now a company builds up a net profit, say 7% to build the price. So price P \= 1.07 C. For example if Rs 100 is the total cost of a particular item, the corresponding price is Rs 107 quoted to the client. Remember, in the price of Rs 107, Rs 100 is the cost which is 93.45% of the price (100/107 \= 93.45%).
    13→
    14→Assume that the standard client payment terms are (1) 10% Advance for mobilisation against a Advance Bank Guarantee of like amount (2) 80% progressive payment against submission of bills for works done and (3) Retention money of 10% which may be released on completion of the project against a Bank Guarantee of like amount valid till the end of Defects Liability Period (DLP).
    15→
    16→From above terms, when you bill for a work done worth for say Rs 100, then the following are the mathematics you should remember:
    17→
    18→Work done worth \= Rs 100 (billed to client)  
    19→Pro rata advance already received before commencement of work \= Rs 10 (10% advance)  
    20→Retention payment \= Rs 10 (10% retention), to be received after completion of the project, as explained above  
    21→Progressive payment received \= Rs 8 (80% progressive payment)  
    22→Total money received \= Rs 10 (Advance) \+ Rs 80 (progressive) \= Rs 90  
    23→Total Cost \= 93.45% of Rs 100, ie. Rs 93.45
    24→
    25→Did you observe one important critical data? While you receive Rs 90 in cash (during the course of execution of the project) for a particular item, you spend Rs 93.45 (assuming you have back to back terms with vendors). Therefore you are already in short of money to the tune of Rs 3.45 which has to come from bank loans which in turn have interest costs built up in the cost of project.
    26→
    27→Though you observe that the cost Rs 93.45 is greater than the revenue of Rs 90, this is not a loss because you will receive a further Rs 10 against this work done later on when you complete the project (ie. the retention money). This means that the profit of Rs 7 is still there but embedded in the retention money. In other words, you earn the profits when the retention amount is paid to you. However in the process, you have observed that there is a shortage of cash during execution because of the retention money held by the client.
    28→
    29→This cost of Rs 93.45 can be disbursed to vendors and paid for overheads by utilizing the revenue of Rs 90 and the balance Rs 3.45 taken as bank loans. However, if certain portion of this revenue of Rs 90 earned by the SBU is transferred to other SBU by the CFO, then the SBU falls further short of cash. 
    30→
    31→In the process, the SBU cannot pay to its vendors on time for the supplies made by them. In turn, they mistrust the contractor for not paying on time and subsequently they tend not to cooperate with the organization in future tenders, or they load more finance costs in their offers to compensate for anticipated delayed payments making bids higher priced. Such higher priced bids may fail to win in the competition and the organisation suffers.
    32→
    33→What a CFO should do in such cases? Wherever a SBU is not performing well or is losing money, the first step is to go for bank loans to run the project. Never take away money from a performing SBU which does not have a cash surplus to a non performing SBU, otherwise sooner or later the performing SBU may  become a non performing SBU. Every SBU should be treated as an independent company with its own balance sheet and that’s the basic concept of creating a SBU. Once you mix finances of different SBUs, the performing SBUs will go for a toss.
    34→
    35→**The salient points of concern are:**
    36→
    37→1. Do not finance a non performing SBU from the revenues of a performing SBU. Keep finances separate.  
    38→2. Transfer of funds across SBUs may be done only if a SBU has cash surplus.   
    39→3. Treat each SBU as different independent organisations so far finance is considered.
    40→

1→---
     2→title: "NO KNOWLEDGE OF HEDGING"
     3→part: 19
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3362-3379"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NO KNOWLEDGE OF HEDGING**
     9→
    10→Often tenders are floated on fixed price basis. That is, the bidder have to quote for prices of materials and services on fixed cost basis which means the prices quoted will remain fixed right from the time of submission of the bid through the bid evaluation period, order process, and the completion period of the project. This time frame may range from 2 years to 4 years depending on the nature, vastness and complexity of the project. For example, if a bid validity is 6 months and project execution period is 18 months, then the prices of a fixed price bid must be kept fixed for the period of 24 months.
    11→
    12→In such a case, many products comprising of steel, zinc, aluminium  
    13→and copper, etc and also the labour cost of subcontractors may be subject to huge escalations during the said time frame.
    14→
    15→So how do you arrive at the cost which has to remain fixed till the completion of the project? Very difficult, I must admit because it’s almost impossible to estimate the likely escalations of the materials and services during this time frame. Some resort to past indications; say for example, you study the fluctuation of cost of zinc during the past five years and note the increase of the raw material cost from year to year. Though you may get some idea, the results may be very confusing. In a particular year, the increase may be significant, in another year it can be constant, in other words, the graph of increase will not be linear. It may be like the graph of a stock in the stock market fluctuation every now and then. Also the fluctuations in the recent past one year may not be representative of the fluctuation that will likely happen going forward.  
    16→   
    17→It is sometimes observed that CFOs may be beating their drums of their position and performance but have no clue to solve such problems. The answer to this is hedging which of course may not be hundred percent correct. Therefore there must be a senior employee in the Finance team having knowledge to hedge, either through banks or concerned agencies or even try to estimate the likely fluctuations of materials and services through economic reports published by various agencies. 
    18→
    19→I will not explain what hedging is, but just for the benefit of young professionals, it is method of insurance with a bank or an agency to fix the likely fluctuation in costs against a premium. This premium now becomes a cost of the bid but it needs to be considered as a cost to mitigate the risk of fluctuations. Just like a life insurance premium is a cost but it is to mitigate the risk your family carries in the event of loss of your life.
    20→
    21→**The salient points are:**
    22→
    23→1. A senior finance person must be entrusted with the job of hedging. He can guide all SBUs to de-risk cost fluctuations of materials and services in fixed price contracts.  
    24→2. Studying past fluctuations of material and service costs may give someone a rough idea but it’s dangerous to presume that the pattern would continue in a similar manner in the future.
    25→

1→---
     2→title: "NO KNOWLEDGE OF INTERNATIONAL LINES OF CREDIT"
     3→part: 19
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3380-3395"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NO KNOWLEDGE OF INTERNATIONAL LINES OF CREDIT**
     9→
    10→Suppose a tender or an opportunity calls for a project requiring financing. So you have two broad components of cost, one is the cost of the project itself comprising of direct costs, indirect costs and project management costs, and the other is the cost of financing the project. Now, we are talking about the latter.
    11→
    12→Depending on the viability of the project, many banks may agree to finance the project in their own terms leading to different costs of finance. However, there are some agencies which also offer credit to projects requiring finance called the Lines of Credit. Lines of Credit are extended by the Export Import Bank of India (Exim Bank) and many other such agencies around the world.
    13→
    14→In India, most tenders, almost all, is awarded based on the cheapest responsive bid; that is the lowest priced bid, subject to meeting certain minimum qualifications and conditions in terms of your past experience records, financials, organisation infrastructure, equipment & machinery availability, etc. Similarly, if a project needs to be financed, you need to find the cheapest Lines of Credit which will provide the cheapest finance cost.
    15→
    16→Sometimes you may find that neither the CFO nor any of the Finance Heads of different SBUs are experienced enough to find the right Line of Credit, particularly from other countries providing such finance simply because of lack of knowledge. Therefore knowledge of financing projects in the international domain is very important if the organisation has a vision to expand such business which usually provides much higher margins in the market than the other stereo typed tenders and projects.
    17→
    18→**The salient points are:**
    19→
    20→1. Lines of Credit are extended by many agencies around the world for financing projects. Such Lines of Credit are sought by clients who either have no finance to execute a project through contractors or are not willing to finance projects because they want to utilize their money for something else. EXIM Bank is one of those agencies in India.  
    21→2. Usually projects financed through Lines of Credit yield higher margins than normal EPC projects.  
    22→3. The contractor needs to comply with the terms and conditions set by the agency extending the Line of Credit for financing a project
    23→

1→---
     2→title: "NO MONITORING OF UBR (UNBILLED REVENUES)"
     3→part: 19
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3396-3415"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NO MONITORING OF UBR (UNBILLED REVENUES)**
     9→
    10→What are unbilled revenues? They are part of turnover which may be considered in the books of accounts but cannot be billed to the client. So it’s a kind of inventory at site. Let me explain with an example.
    11→
    12→Suppose you have quoted Rs 20 Lakhs for the casting of a foundation of a structure for which the corresponding cost is Rs 15 Lakhs. This activity comprises of many elements, ie. designs to be completed and approved by client, cement/sand/reinforcement steel/coarse aggregates/water to be supplied at site, concrete mixers and other equipment to be available at site and finally the labour who will cast the foundation. Now suppose, at the end of a month or a quarter, you have supplied the cement and sand but other materials and equipment might not have arrived at site. Even if all materials and equipment are available, maybe the labour have not yet cast the foundation till the last day of the month. So you may have incurred 80% of Rs 15 Lakhs (ie. Rs 12 Lakhs, say), as the cost but could not complete the end product, that is, the foundation. Therefore you can book this 80% amount as revenues but cannot book the corresponding profits or bill this Rs 12 Lkahs to the client. As you complete the work for the balance 20% at site (ie. Rs 15 Lakhs – Rs 12 Lakhs \= balance Rs 3 Lakhs) in the beginning of next month, that is, the casting of foundation is over, only then you can bill Rs 20 Lakhs to the client and book the balance Rs  8 Lakhs (Rs 20 Lakhs – Rs 12 Lakhs \= Rs 8 Lakhs) in turnover and also book Rs 5 Lakhs (Rs 20 Lakhs – Rs 15 Lakhs \= Rs 5 Lakhs) as profit in the books of accounts.
    13→
    14→In the above example, Rs 12 Lakhs is the UBR and well understood. However, since many Finance personnel are not acquainted with the technicalities at site, sometimes the Project Manager may misinform certain loss making activities as UBR. Some examples of these are:
    15→
    16→1. A foundation could have been cast earlier and the company may have received the money already against the submitted bill. Later on, the client may have supervised to find bad quality work and may have issued an order for re-work. Such a re-work may have been concealed by the Site Manager. The Finance guy may not know that the cement and steel and other materials being sent now is for the re-work of the same foundation cast and billed earlier but was found to be defective by the client. Therefore this time after the re-work the foundation cast will not be billable once again. To conceal such bad quality work, the cost of these materials for re-work is wrongly declared as UBR but actually these are unbillable\! This amount is shown as UBR over the years, never to be seen as revenues, and ultimately booked as loss.    
    17→     
    18→2. Say in a structure built at site, some components have been stolen. Say, the Site Manager was careless and may not have filed a FIR for theft. Now at the end of the project, upon client’s supervision, client orders to replenish such stolen components. The payment for this structure might have already been billed and received by the company. Now the Project Manager arranges supplies of these components. Again, to conceal such carelessness, the Site Manager declares this supply cost as UBR. But actually the structure is being rectified. So basically this so called UBR is actually unbillable\! This amount shown as UBR is reflected over the years in the books, never to be seen as revenues, and ultimately booked as loss.  
    19→
    20→The Finance must have checks and balances of all materials and services and each such cost tagged to each item of billing so that any further materials are sent is not found to be for the same item for which payment has been received earlier. Upon such enquiries, the Finance can always find out that materials being sent are to be accounted as “Loss” or UBR.
    21→
    22→**The salient points are:**
    23→
    24→1. The Project manager and the Finance Head must have complete checks and balances to understand whether the Site Manager’s UBR is truly a UBR or a loss.  
    25→2. Each UBR must be tagged with a specific work and must be tracked as to when the UBR is converted to revenues. There must be targets of time frames given against each UBR for its conversion to revenues.  
    26→3. UBR being extended for months are suspicious and requires investigation.
    27→

     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : WRONG BILLING**
     9→
    10→Sometimes, Finance team through its Commercial and Billing team may produce wrong documents inadvertently to the client and therefore the bill gets stuck at client’s side. There is no issue to make mistakes at times since “to err is human”. But the problem is when such bill pending for a long time is ignored by Finance. Finance Head must keep a track of bills raised and corresponding payments received. Also I have observed that the Finance team does not form a habit to meet the Finance departments of the clients. They should keep a good professional relationship with them in the interest of the organization.
    11→
    12→These wrong bills submitted to the client account for the turnover of the organization but it sincerely affects the cash flow due to non-receipt of such payments on time. 
    13→

    16→1. The Finance Head must have a spreadsheet indicating billes raised and corresponding payments received.  
    17→2. To receive payments on time or rectifying wrong bills as early as possible, the Finance team must maintain a good professional relationship with the Finance team of the client.

     7→
     8→**AUDIT**
     9→
    10→**Role of Audit in a corporate**  
    11→Audits play a vital role in corporate settings by ensuring financial accuracy, build transparency, and accountability. They help detect fraud, strengthen internal controls, and stakeholder trust.
    12→
    13→Audits verify the reliability of financial statements and assess risk management systems. They ensure compliance with laws and regulations while identifying errors or irregularities. This independent review supports informed decision-making for executives and investors.
    14→
    15→Internal audits evaluate operations, controls, and efficiency to improve processes. External audits provide an unbiased opinion on financial statements for shareholders and regulators
    16→
    17→Audits enhance credibility of financial reporting and protect stakeholder interests. They reduce risks like fraud and promote ethical governance practices. Strong audits also aid resource allocation and long-term business success.
    18→
    19→Every business needs checks and balances to ensure that there is no misuse or misappropriation of funds or manipulated records of supplies and services.
    20→
    21→There may two kinds of Audit; one is the Financial Audit and the other the Technical Audit. While Financial audit provide checks and balances in funds, receipts, disbursement, loans, guarantees, cash flows, lines of credit, revenues,  profit and books of accounts, etc, Technical Audit concentrates on engineering practices, quality and quantity of products and services used, orders, agreements, progress of projects, quality and safety in projects.
    22→
    23→Auditors usually act as an interface to the SBU and the Top Leadership. They sit and discuss facts and figures with the Heads of BU, SBU, Finance and report to their Leaders on their findings with proposal of improvements. Also they point out any malpractices being adopted during the course of business. 
    24→
    25→Sometimes an organization may also appoint an External Auditor for the purpose so that the Management receives unbiased reports, however, this may be in addition to an Internal Auditor.   
    26→The basic purpose of Audit is to ensure that an organization is conducting business in an ethical manner with established good practices following governance model of the organization and the laws of the country.
    27→
    28→Experience auditors can guide the business with their findings so that the business heads can course correct in their operations. Auditors act as facilitators to ethical and law abiding business and also act as a catalyst to improvements in business.
    29→

    10→A Manager responsible for a Technical Audit must have knowledge of the basics of the technicalities involved in the business so that he can understand the ethical process on how a business should be conducted and how and what records must be maintained for an effective MIS. Therefore the Manager should have engineering or a technical background to do justice to his responsibility.

    12→Likewise, a Manager responsible for a Financial Audit must have knowledge of the basics of Finance so that he can understand how and what records are being kept from time to time. These records will also form a part of the Annual Audited Reports and hence its importance of correctness for the Management as well as the shareholders.
    13→
    14→However, I have often seen that only one Manager has been kept for the Technical and Financial Audit, maybe to save cost, most often having a finance background, but having an extreme lack of knowledge in technical matters. Again, to prove his work, he lists such technical disparities, found out as per his opinion, which are not significant or important to be reported. An auditor must be reasonable competent on technical matters involved in the organization.   
    15→    
    16→**The salient points are:**
    17→
    18→1. An auditor may be given an all-round training, particularly on the basics of technical issues and engineering, so that he is armed with the knowledge to find out what is going wrong in the corporate or at sites.  
    19→2. An auditor and the Finance team members can go through the course “Non Finance for Finance”

     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 20
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3488-3504"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Train the Auditor**  
    11→Anyone entrusted with a job may lack knowledge or ideas in a particular sphere of business. There is nothing to be guilty for this. Even a doctor sometimes fails to understand the symptoms of a patient, but that does not mean that the doctor is bad. Similarly, businesses can also be complex and it requires experience and patience to learn. If an Auditor is from a financial background, then he can be trained by a senior professional on the basics of technical processes involved in the business. After acquiring these basics, the Auditor may be in a good position to audit the technical personnel.
    12→
    13→**Maintain harmony**  
    14→The Auditor must convey to the personnel that the audits are basically not intended to find faults in the system, but to point out the irregularities so that the organization can act and rectify those in future. The technical and the Finance Heads must appreciate that the faults and discrepancies found in the system by the Auditor actually helps the organization to correct them and fall in line with SOP and statutory acts. 
    15→
    16→**Set automatic alerts for audits**  
    17→Often auditors miss their dates of audit as planned in the beginning of the year.  This leads to delayed actions on the findings of audit.
    18→
    19→**Updated checklists**  
    20→Sometimes auditors carry on their audits based on outdated checklists. Updated checklists will ensure correctness of modern methods being employed by the organization in all the fields of operations.
    21→
    22→**Only citing problems, not suggesting solutions**

     2→title: "FIGHTING WITH BU TEAM"

     4→section: 4

     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : FIGHTING WITH BU TEAM**
     9→
    10→Sometimes I have seen difference of opinions voiced between the Auditor and the Technical/Finance personnel. In such cases, the Auditor may mention points of view from both the sides in its report and leave it to the Management to act on what is right.
    11→
    12→However, sometimes such difference of opinions flares up into arguments. And to demonstrate his power and good work, the Auditor tries to list all the observed discrepancies (according to him), big or small, right or wrong, and report to the Management expecting the top to act against the Finance/Technical personnel. The Auditor may also have the permission to submit his report to the Managing Director with a copy to the BU/SBU Head so that the SBU Head is not in a position to manipulate the report in a favourable manner. Sometimes the Auditor derives sadistic pleasure in thinking that the SBU Head or the Finance/Technical personnel will be reprimanded based on his audit report.
    13→
    14→Such situations lead to disharmony between Auditors and the business team. Both parties must have a positive outlook to understand and appreciate what is being done is right or wrong. An honest BU/SBU Head must appreciate to rectify or improve processes as reported by the Auditor. After all, the organization not only benefits from the entire process but also ensures that all its data and processes are in line with statutory acts of the country. An Auditor should act like a facilitator to the business.
    15→
    16→**The salient points are:**
    17→
    18→1. An auditor should act as a facilitator to business. Minor lapses may be corrected by the concerned personnel. Bit major lapses must be reported to the top so that the top can strictly warn the concerned to correct themselves in future  
    19→2. An auditor can go through an all-round training in business operations so that he has the right knowledge to decide on his findings.

20→Whatever is the case, the subject matter of this chapter is about half yearly appraisals which are often not done. Then during the annual appraisals, the half yearly appraisals of the employees are put in place with cooked up data. Why cooked up?
